Dark heads rise and vanish among the waves beside the hull. The raiders climb with hooks when spray hides the first splash.
A slick of blood from some unseen kill trails across the swell. Reef sharks follow it toward anything thrashing in the water.
A heavy thump rolls under the boat as thick arms curl around the keel. The octopus squeezes from below while the sea rocks above.
The sea lies smooth as hammered metal, but low ripples cross against the wind. Reading them reveals the start of a dangerous current.
A single wave rises from the wrong direction and slams hard across the deck or rocks. Loose gear and loose footing both become hazards.
At night the wake glows green with sparks of living light. Each oar stroke or wave slap scatters stars through the black water.
A torn signal flag floats beside a patch of wreckage. Its pattern marks distress, but the nearest ship is nowhere in sight.
A sealed bottle bobs in a tangle of weed, its message written in a hand shaking from haste. The coordinates point off the known route.
A warning buoy drifts far from its anchor, its bell packed with seaweed. Resetting it could keep the next ship off the shoals.
A wide mat of kelp rolls beside the current, sheltering tiny crabs and edible fronds. The freshest blades sit near the center.
Air-filled weed carries little berry-like bladders prized for bitter stews. Sorting them before they burst takes a light touch.
A cluster of coconuts and palm debris floats in the wake of a distant storm. The good nuts knock hollow and low.
A school of mackerel breaks the surface in silver bursts. Fast lines can bring in several before larger hunters scatter them.
Diving birds mark a tuna run just ahead of the swell. Reaching it in time means reading the flock more than the water.
An old lobster pot drags along a shallow reef, still baited and still catching. Recovering it risks scraping hull or boots on coral.
A raft of pale pumice stones drifts across the swell after a distant eruption. The light stones can be scooped before they spread too thin.
A shallow shoal glimmers with oyster shells below clear water. Taking pearls without tearing the bed apart requires care.
A waxy gray lump floats among gull feathers and weed. Identifying it before the sea takes it again turns refuse into value.
A broken mast drifts just below the surface, dangerous to hulls but useful if hauled aboard. Its rigging tangles around everything nearby.
Wreckage from a shattered deck rides the swell in a loose fan. The longest planks can be hooked before the current spreads them out.
Long kelp stems twist together like rope beneath a floating mat. Properly dried, they make strong lashings for temporary repairs.
A dark lateen sail appears where the horizon was empty moments before. Grapples fly as the reavers close under a false merchant flag.
A waterlogged sailor clings to the rail asking for help in three voices. When pulled aboard, it unfolds claws and a mouth full of barnacles.
Tiny storm-spirits dance along wet rigging and laugh when lightning answers. Their first burst sends sparks across every metal fitting.
Clouds break long enough to reveal familiar Sword Coast stars. A clean sighting corrects the course before currents carry the ship wide.
A heavy green sea climbs the bow and sweeps the deck clean. Grabbing fast keeps gear and bodies from vanishing overboard.
Moonlight opens a path of silver across calm water. A few quiet minutes under it eases bruises and seasick bones.
Ghost light off the starboard bow
A pale ship-light burns where no vessel appears on the charts. Each night it draws closer and spells a different name in foam.
A drifting cutter has no crew, but the captain's cabin is locked from the inside and the log is still wet with fresh ink.
A navigation buoy sinks and rises as if breathing. Its message tube contains coordinates and a request signed by a dead harbor master.
A hooked shark carries a waxed chart case tied under its fin. The chart marks a cove, a tide, and the words 'no lanterns'.
A ship's bell rings from beneath glassy water whenever the deck falls silent. Divers who answer it find a trail of silver coins.
Clusters of sea grapes cling to floating sargasso mats. The ripe ones are sweet, but the mat hides stinging threads.
Fresh stormweed twists around a floating spar after rough weather. It dries into useful cord and bitter tea.
A bundle of coconuts bobs in a net from some lost island canoe. Cutting the net wrong sends half the cache away.
Flying fish burst from the waves and patter across the deck like rain. Fast hands and baskets turn surprise into supper.
Gulls scream over a blue patch where tuna drive baitfish upward. A strong line and steady pull matter most.
Squid rise around lantern light after midnight. The best catch comes before something larger follows the glow.
A floating wreck has spilled carved coral ballast stones. Their smooth weight is valuable if hauled before they sink.
A cracked wreck bell shows green copper under barnacles. Cutting a usable sheet free takes tools and a calm deck.
A raft of pale pumice floats after distant volcanic fire. The light stones scoop easily but scatter on rough water.
A mast length floats low in the swell, waterlogged but still straight. Securing it requires a line before the sea turns it broadside.
Broken cask staves drift in an oily slick. The cleanest pieces can be scrubbed and reused for small repairs.
An oar of good ash wood is tangled in sargasso and old rope. Pulling it loose without breaking the blade takes careful cuts.
